questions.question: Explain the purpose of try and catch blocks in Java exception handling. How do they work together?
keyPoints: Try block encloses code that might throw an exception
Catch block handles the thrown exception
When exception occurs in try, execution transfers to catch
Exception object is passed to catch for information
Normal execution continues after catch block completes

questions.question: Why is the order of catch blocks important when handling multiple exception types?
keyPoints: More specific exceptions must come before more general ones
FileNotFoundException before IOException
RuntimeException before Exception
Compiler error if unreachable catch block exists
Ensures proper exception handling based on specificity

questions.question: What is the purpose of the finally block? When does it execute and when does it not execute?
keyPoints: Finally block always executes regardless of exception
Used for cleanup operations (closing files, releasing resources)
Executes even if catch block throws exception
Does NOT execute if System.exit() is called
Does NOT execute if JVM crashes or Runtime.halt() called

questions.question: What are the methods available in the Exception object to get information about the exception?
keyPoints: getMessage() - returns detail message string
toString() - returns class and message
printStackTrace() - prints stack trace to stderr
getStackTrace() - returns StackTraceElement array
getCause() - returns underlying cause exception

questions.question: What is the difference between checked and unchecked exceptions? How does this affect exception handling with try-catch?
keyPoints: Checked: subclasses of Exception not RuntimeException
Unchecked: RuntimeException and its subclasses
Checked must be caught or declared with throws
Unchecked don't require mandatory handling
Both can be caught using try-catch blocks

questions.question: Write a Java program that demonstrates nested try-catch blocks. Include proper exception handling for ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException and ArithmeticException within a nested structure. Explain the flow of execution.
keyPoints: Outer try-catch wraps multiple operations
Inner try-catch handles specific exceptions
Shows exception propagation from inner to outer
Demonstrates both catch blocks catching different exceptions
Code should compile and run demonstrating both exception types

questions.question: Explain try-with-resources in Java. How does it simplify exception handling and resource management? Write a code example demonstrating its use.
keyPoints: AutoCloseable interface requirement
Automatic resource closing at end of try block
Multiple resources can be declared with semicolons
Suppressed exceptions feature
Code is cleaner than try-finally for resource management

questions.question: What is multi-catch in Java? Explain its syntax and when it should be used. Write a code example demonstrating the handling of multiple exception types with a single catch block.
keyPoints: Introduced in Java 7
Syntax: catch (Exception1 | Exception2 e)
Used when multiple exceptions need same handling
Pipe operator separates exception types
Reduces code duplication for similar exception handling
